moment_text: Maybe 10, 11, 12 years old. During that time, I was noticing that my peers, many of them were. Something different was happening in my body and that I was not moving through the same things as they were, you know, 10, 11, 12, 13. Growing up I was extremely upset and worried, um, because I had not had a period, um, my period was not on on time. Um, it took me several years to get a first period. Um, and around that time I was also starting to notice other, what I have now recognized as hormonal symptoms. Back During that time, I was really, really overweight. It was really hard for me to lose weight. At that time. I noticed that my peers, right, like we were all eating maybe similar foods, but it, it just seemed as though. They were metabolizing the food a bit differently. Um, I had terrible acne. Um, and I know that that can be a familiar experience across many young folks, but I just had a deep gnawing feeling that something was really amiss here. Um, I was constantly hungry, constantly hot, um, and my period was just. Pretty much irregular. 14, 15, 16, those years when I was in high school, um, really began. My desperate attempt to be understood and listened to by doctors. Um, it was around that time that I was going to multiple OBGYNs and multiple providers trying to get them to listen to me and trying to get them to understand what was actually happening in my body. moment_text: Providers just kept. Shrugging me off, gaslighting me that that's where one of my first experiences of what medical gaslighting can look like, right? Why providers were so quick to tell me that everything was fine and my blood work looked fine and nothing needed to be done, and it's okay to not get, you know, your cycle. That's really the messaging that I was heard in a lot of ways that I was told in a lot of ways, and so. Throughout this time, I think that was really my first inflection point. To be honest. It was my first up close and personal barring and contending with the medical industrial complex as we know it today, and being a young person. Spending a lot of time, spending a lot of resources that I didn't already have, spending a lot of mental energy, trying to get folks to believe me and to affirm my experience. Um, and it wouldn't be until 10 years later that I would get a diagnosis of PCOS and finally begin to start treatment. But all in those 10 years, my health was progressively deteriorating. What now became irregular periods? Soon Metamorpho, you know, metamorpho into. Fatty liver disease into all of these different things. Um, had I been taken seriously in the first place, had I had been, um, given a platform to really express and to expand upon my worries that were very important to me and deeply personal to me, perhaps I would've had access to treatment much earlier. And this did not stop, you know, this persisted for many years until I finally got the right diagnosis and was offered substantial medical treatment beyond birth control, right beyond the birth control pill.
